title: Articles and gender
rule_text: Nouns are usually masculine or feminine. Common pattern: nouns ending in -o are often masculine and nouns ending in -a are often feminine. Use definite articles el, la, los, las and indefinite articles un, una, unos, unas. Adjectives often agree with the noun in gender and number.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong article with a noun
Forgetting adjective agreement

title: Singular and plural
rule_text: Add -s after a vowel, add -es after most consonants, and change z to c before adding -es.
common_mistakes: Adding the wrong plural ending
Not changing z to c

title: Subject pronouns
rule_text: Spanish subject pronouns are yo, tu, el, ella, usted, nosotros, nosotras, vosotros, vosotras, ellos, ellas, and ustedes. Spanish often drops the subject pronoun when the verb form is clear.
common_mistakes: Overusing subject pronouns in every sentence

title: Present tense basics
rule_text: Regular present tense endings are -o, -as, -a, -amos, -an for -ar verbs; -o, -es, -e, -emos, -en for -er verbs; and -o, -es, -e, -imos, -en for -ir verbs.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong ending for the verb type

title: Ser vs estar
rule_text: Use ser for identity, origin, characteristics, time, and relationships. Use estar for location, temporary states, and many feelings or conditions.
common_mistakes: Using ser for location
Using estar for permanent identity

title: Hay vs estar
rule_text: Use hay to say that something exists. Use estar to say where a specific thing is located.
examples: Hay un libro en la mesa.
El libro esta en la mesa.
common_mistakes: Using esta to introduce existence
Using hay for location of a specific object

title: Question words
rule_text: Common question words include que, quien, donde, cuando, por que, como, and cual.
common_mistakes: Choosing the wrong interrogative word

title: Gustar pattern
rule_text: Gustar works like 'is pleasing to'. Use gusta with a singular noun or infinitive and gustan with plural nouns.
common_mistakes: Treating gustar like English like
Using gusta with plural nouns

title: Negation
rule_text: Put no before the verb. Words like nunca, nadie, and nada also commonly appear with no before the verb.
common_mistakes: Putting no in the wrong position

title: Adjective agreement
rule_text: Adjectives usually match the noun in gender and number.
common_mistakes: Leaving adjectives in masculine singular form

title: Possessives
rule_text: Common possessives are mi, tu, su, nuestro, and vuestro. They agree in number when needed, such as mis libros and nuestras casas.
common_mistakes: Forgetting plural agreement in possessives

title: Direct object pronouns
rule_text: Direct object pronouns replace the noun receiving the action: me, te, lo, la, nos, os, los, las.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong object pronoun
Leaving the noun and pronoun together unnecessarily

title: Indirect object pronouns
rule_text: Indirect object pronouns show to whom or for whom something is done: me, te, le, nos, os, les.
examples: Doy el libro a Ana. -> Le doy el libro.
common_mistakes: Confusing direct and indirect object pronouns

title: Common present irregulars
rule_text: Common present tense irregulars include tengo, hago, pongo, salgo, vengo, digo, oigo, and conozco.
common_mistakes: Applying regular endings to irregular first-person forms

title: Stem-changing verbs
rule_text: Common stem changes are e to ie, o to ue, and e to i. The change usually appears in all present tense forms except nosotros and vosotros.
common_mistakes: Applying the stem change to nosotros forms

title: Reflexive verbs
rule_text: Reflexive verbs use me, te, se, nos, os, and se with the verb and are common for routines and self-directed actions.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the reflexive pronoun

title: Near future
rule_text: Use ir + a + infinitive to express 'going to' in the near future.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the preposition a

title: Simple past overview
rule_text: Use the preterite for completed actions. Use the imperfect for background, repeated past actions, age, time, and ongoing situations.
examples: Cuando era nino, estudiaba en casa.
common_mistakes: Using preterite when describing background context
Using imperfect for a clearly completed action

title: Por vs para
rule_text: Por often expresses cause, exchange, movement through, duration, or means. Para often expresses purpose, destination, deadline, or recipient.
common_mistakes: Using por when expressing purpose
Using para when expressing reason or exchange

title: Personal a
rule_text: Use a before a specific human direct object.
common_mistakes: Omitting the personal a with people

title: Comparisons
rule_text: Use mas ... que for 'more than', menos ... que for 'less than', and tan ... como for 'as ... as'. Mejor and peor are irregular comparisons.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong comparison pattern

title: Commands overview
rule_text: Informal tu commands are common: habla, come, vive. Negative tu commands use the present subjunctive: no hables, no comas, no vivas.
common_mistakes: Using affirmative tu forms in negative commands
